output:
## Yes — Campaign B maintains effective recall longer despite starting at half the initial rate.

Solving R(t) = 20 for each campaign:

**t = τ · ln(R₀ / 20)**

| | R₀ | τ | t where R(t) = 20% | 
|---|---|---|---|
| **Campaign A** (TV) | 80% | 30 days | 30 · ln(4) = **41.6 days** |
| **Campaign B** (digital) | 40% | 90 days | 90 · ln(2) = **62.4 days** |

**Campaign B stays effective ~50% longer** (62.4 vs 41.6 days), gaining about 21 extra days of purchase influence.

### Why this happens

The decay constant τ matters more than the initial recall R₀. Campaign A starts twice as high but decays 3× faster. Mathematically, effective duration scales *linearly* with τ but only *logarithmically* with R₀ — so doubling R₀ adds only τ·ln(2) ≈ 0.69τ days, while tripling τ multiplies the entire duration.

This is the classic reach-vs-retention tradeoff: a smaller but more memorable impression outlasts a louder but forgettable one.
